perturbation.original_text: 14. DISPUTES. In the event of any dispute which arises between the parties and which relates to the subject matter of this Agreement, the parties acknowledge and agree that any such dispute shall be submitted for binding arbitration in accordance with the arbitration procedures established by the American arbitration Association in Denver, Colorado.
perturbation.changed_text: 14. DISPUTES. In the event of any dispute which arises between the parties and which relates to the subject matter of this Agreement, the parties agree that any such dispute may be submitted to arbitration.
perturbation.explanation: By removing 'binding' and the reference to the American Arbitration Association, the contract removes a legally recognized avenue for resolving disputes and weakens consumer protection afforded by structured arbitration processes. This change introduces ambiguity and potentially forces the professional to bear a larger burden in dispute resolution.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Colorado Revised Statutes § 13-22-206 - Proceedings to compel or stay arbitration
perturbation.law_explanation: The original clause specifies 'binding arbitration', which is legally enforceable under Colorado law. By omitting this, along with the reference to the American Arbitration Association's procedures, the modified text contradicts the legal framework that provides a structured process for compelling arbitration. CRS § 13-22-206 outlines the proceedings related to compelling or staying arbitration, which assumes a legally binding agreement to arbitrate. The modified text weakens this enforceability.

perturbation.justification: The modified text removes the 'binding' requirement and the reference to the American Arbitration Association (AAA). The Law Explanation states that the original clause specifies 'binding arbitration,' which is legally enforceable under Colorado law. Omitting this, along with the AAA reference, weakens the enforceability of the agreement to arbitrate. The Perturbation Explanation supports this by noting that the removal weakens consumer protection. Although the scraped snippets are unhelpful, the analysis of the original and modified text, coupled with the Law and Perturbation Explanations, shows that the change removes a legally recognized avenue for dispute resolution.

perturbation.original_text: 7. NOTICE. All notices and communications required or permitted to be given hereunder shall be in writing, signed by the sender, and delivered by registered or certified mail to: If to the Company: Robert G. Tomlinson, Chairman and CEO Sport-Haley, Inc. 4600 East 48th Avenue Denver, CO 80216 With a copy to: Robert W. Walter, Esq. Berliner Zisser Walter & Gallegos, P. C. Suite 4700 1700 Lincoln Street Denver, CO 80203 If to the Professional: With a copy to: or such other address as shall have been furnished in writing by the parties to each other.
perturbation.changed_text: 7. NOTICE. All notices and communications required or permitted to be given hereunder shall be delivered to the addresses provided by the parties.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the requirement for 'written, signed by the sender, and delivered by registered or certified mail' and instead simply stating 'delivered to the addresses provided by the parties', the contract introduces ambiguity and removes the assurance of proof of delivery. This could violate laws that require specific methods of notification for contractual changes or disputes.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Colorado Uniform Electronic Transactions Act, CRS 24-71.3-101 et seq.
perturbation.law_explanation: CRS 24-71.3-108 addresses the attribution of electronic records and signatures. While it allows for electronic notifications, removing the explicit requirements for written, signed, and certified delivery creates uncertainty regarding whether electronic methods comply. Without specifying standards for authentication and receipt verification, the modified text could contradict the intent of the UETA, which provides a framework for electronic transactions to be considered reliable and legally binding.

perturbation.justification: The original contract mandates specific methods for delivering notices (written, signed, registered/certified mail), ensuring proof of delivery. The changed text simplifies this to 'delivered to the addresses provided,' omitting these safeguards. The Law Explanation suggests this removal creates uncertainty regarding compliance with laws governing electronic records and signatures, potentially contradicting the intent of ensuring reliable and legally binding transactions. While the scraped snippets are unhelpful, the perturbation explanation highlights that the deletion of proof of delivery methods weakens the legal defensibility of the clause. This falls under Omission Legal Contradiction because it removes essential information about proof of delivery, thus creating legal loopholes by potentially making it more difficult to prove that notice was properly given under the contract.

perturbation.original_text: 6. TERM. The term of this Agreement shall extend from the date of execution hereof through and until _______, unless extended by written agreement of the parties. The parties shall use their best efforts to reach agreement on the terms of any such extension. In the event the Professional should for any reason become a non-exempt PGA Tour player, the Company shall have the right to terminate this Agreement at its discretion at any time during the initial term or any extension thereof. Additionally, if either part shall fail to observe or perform any of the agreements or obligations undertaken by such party hereunder, and such failure or default shall continue for a period of 30 days following notice from the non-defaulting party to the defaulting party during which such failure or default shall not have been cured by the defaulting party, then the non-defaulting party shall have the right to terminate this Agreement following the expiration of such 30-day notice period.
perturbation.changed_text: 6. TERM. The term of this Agreement shall extend from the date of execution hereof through and until _______, unless extended by agreement of the parties. In the event the Professional should for any reason become a non-exempt PGA Tour player, the Company shall have the right to terminate this Agreement at its discretion at any time during the initial term or any extension thereof. Additionally, if either part shall fail to observe or perform any of the agreements or obligations undertaken by such party hereunder, the non-defaulting party shall have the right to terminate this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: The original text includes a 30-day cure period after notice of default. By removing this cure period, the contract becomes more stringent and potentially unfair to the professional, conflicting with general principles of good faith and fair dealing implied in contracts. Colorado law recognizes the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing, which this modification could arguably violate.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 205 - Duty of Good Faith and Fair Dealing
perturbation.law_explanation: While not a statute, Colorado courts often rely on the Restatement (Second) of Contracts, specifically Section 205, which implies a duty of good faith and fair dealing in every contract. By removing the 30-day cure period, the modified text allows for immediate termination upon any breach, regardless of severity or ability to rectify, which could be interpreted as a violation of the implied duty of good faith.
perturbation.justification: The modification removes a 30-day cure period for defaults, allowing immediate termination upon any breach. This alteration arguably violates the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing, as highlighted in the Law Explanation referencing Restatement (Second) of Contracts, Section 205. Even without specific law snippets, the removal of the cure period creates a situation where the contract's enforcement could be seen as unduly harsh and not in good faith, creating an Omission Legal Contradiction by removing essential consumer protections.
